sentences.base_language: Hello! I'd like to sign up for a membership.
sentences.target_language: 你好！我想办一张健身卡。
sentences.romanized: Nǐ hǎo! Wǒ xiǎng bàn yī zhāng jiànshēn kǎ.
sentences.grammarNote: **办卡** (bàn kǎ) = to get / sign up for a card. **办** is the go-to verb for processing or obtaining official documents and memberships: 办签证 (get a visa), 办手续 (handle procedures).

sentences.base_language: Great! We offer monthly and annual memberships. Which would you prefer?
sentences.target_language: 好的！我们有月卡和年卡，您想办哪种？
sentences.romanized: Hǎo de! Wǒmen yǒu yuè kǎ hé nián kǎ, nín xiǎng bàn nǎ zhǒng?
sentences.grammarNote: **月卡** (yuè kǎ) = monthly card; **年卡** (nián kǎ) = annual card. Chinese compounds time + card: **月** (month) / **年** (year) + **卡** (card). **哪种** (nǎ zhǒng) = which kind/type — **种** (zhǒng) is the measure word for types and varieties.

sentences.base_language: How much is the monthly card?
sentences.grammarNote: **多少钱** (duōshǎo qián) = how much money? The most common price question. You can drop 钱 in casual speech: 月卡多少？ Note: **多少** is used for larger or unknown quantities; **几** is for small numbers (under 10).

sentences.base_language: The monthly card is two hundred and eighty yuan. The annual card is two thousand four hundred.
sentences.target_language: 月卡两百八十块，年卡两千四百块。
sentences.romanized: Yuè kǎ liǎng bǎi bāshí kuài, nián kǎ liǎng qiān sìbǎi kuài.
sentences.grammarNote: 年卡 works out to 200 yuan per month versus 280 for monthly. **划算** (huásuàn) = worth it / good value — a very useful word for comparing prices.

sentences.base_language: The annual card is better value. I'll take the annual one.
sentences.target_language: 年卡更划算。我要办年卡。
sentences.romanized: Nián kǎ gèng huásuàn. Wǒ yào bàn nián kǎ.
sentences.grammarNote: **更** (gèng) = even more / more — used for comparisons: 年卡更划算 = the annual card is more worth it. **划算** (huásuàn) = good value / worth it (literally 'calculate worthwhile'). **要** here means 'want to / will' — a decisive statement of intent.

sentences.base_language: Do you also have personal trainers here?
sentences.grammarNote: **私人教练** (sīrén jiàoliàn) = personal trainer: **私人** (private/personal) + **教练** (coach/trainer). **这里有...吗** is a standard existence question: 'Is there … here?' The verb **有** (to have/exist) carries the question.

sentences.base_language: Yes! A trainer costs three hundred yuan per session.
sentences.target_language: 有！私人教练每次三百块。
sentences.romanized: Yǒu! Sīrén jiàoliàn měi cì sānbǎi kuài.
sentences.grammarNote: **次** (cì) as a measure word counts occurrences of an event or session: 每次 = each session, 上了三次课 = attended three classes. Especially common in fitness and class contexts.

sentences.base_language: Maybe I'll try one session to see if I like it.
sentences.target_language: 也许我先试一次，看看喜不喜欢。
sentences.romanized: Yěxǔ wǒ xiān shì yī cì, kàn kàn xǐ bu xǐhuān.
sentences.grammarNote:
**喜不喜欢** is the affirmative-negative question form: 喜欢 + 不 + 喜欢. This A-not-A pattern sounds more natural than adding 吗 in many contexts.
是不是, 好不好, 来不来 — same pattern.

sentences.base_language: No problem! You can start using the gym today.
sentences.target_language: 没问题！今天就可以开始使用了。
sentences.romanized: Méi wèntí! Jīntiān jiù kěyǐ kāishǐ shǐyòng le.
sentences.grammarNote: **没问题** (méi wèntí) = no problem: **没** (not have) + **问题** (problem/question). **就** (jiù) here adds immediacy — 'right away / as soon as today.' The sentence-final **了** signals a new situation: 'you can start now (as of this moment).'

sentences.base_language: Thank you. I'll do my best!
sentences.grammarNote: **加油** (jiāyóu) literally means 'add fuel/oil' but is used universally as encouragement: Go for it! / Keep it up! / You've got this! Shouted at sports events and said to friends facing challenges alike.
